data.title: Why I'm Downgrading Broadcom To A Hold (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

After my buy rating in early January, Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) has dropped over 30% with Trump's tariff headwinds being a key cause. In the below analysis, I determine that most technical signs suggest further weakness in the stock. While there are some near term positive signals in the indicators and weakening bearish momentum in the Ichimoku Clouds, they likely signal a reprieve rather than a true bullish reversal. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were respectable and guidance was satisfactory as growth is expected to remain robust despite some slowdowns. The P/S ratio has contracted to reflect this but remains above average, suggesting slight overvaluation. However, with the company announcing a new share repurchase program, the fundamental setup is likely net positive at this point. Overall, I believe Broadcom has become a hold since while the long term fundamentals may be net attractive, the technicals don't show a bottom yet.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

Broadcom remains in a near term downtrend that has been pressuring the stock since mid-February. The trend line last acted as resistance in late March and given its sharp trajectory, could be worrying about the bulls. While the trend line is quickly becoming the most relevant source of resistance there are three more key levels that I want to discuss. The first is in the mid-160s which represents the recent downside gap in the stock. This area seems to have acted as a bit of resistance on the previous trading day. The next key level is in the mid-180s. This area was a ceiling for the stock from early October to mid-December while being a floor for much of March. Lastly, the round number 200 could be a significant level as it was key support in late January while being resistance in early March.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50 day SMA has remained above its 200-day counterpart for the duration of the past six months, showing that long term bullishness has generally been sustained. However, since late March, the deviation between the SMAs has been decreasing and so momentum has been on the decline. Late last month, the stock also broke beneath the 200-day SMA as an important sign of weakness. As for the EMAs, the 9 day EMA had a bearish crossover with the 21 day EMA in late February to show that the near term had turned bearish for Broadcom. Currently, the gap between the EMAs is significant and shows that near term bearish momentum is still very strong.

The stock was able to break back above the Bollinger Bands midline for a brief period in February, but ultimately, it slid below again. This was an early indication that a downtrend may have been developing. Since crossing back below in late February, the stock has not challenged the midline, and so bearishness has been considerable. Days ago, the stock was below the lower band and so the recent bounce is not surprising as it was oversold. For the MACD, the indicator was unable to break back above the signal line in late March as a sign of persistent bears. Since then, the gap between the lines generally expanded to show a worsening in sentiment until recently when the histogram turned bullish for Broadcom. The MACD did make a new six month low recently, however, confirming the declines in the stock. For the RSI, it currently has a reading of around 36 as it has bounced back from a sub-30 level. The bears are therefore still in control by a wide margin, despite ceding some ground to the bulls. Like the MACD, the RSI confirms the recent plunge in the stock with its own six-month low. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a bullish crossover that just occurred. The %K's trajectory has turned significantly positive, and the gap between the lines seems to be expanding in a healthy manner. The %K has also exited the 20 zone as another sign of weakening bears.

Takeaway

The technical outlook remains bearish overall for Broadcom. The chart shows that the stock remains in a near term downtrend with resistance above, while the moving averages show weakness in both the near and long terms. As for the indicators, most signals were bearish but there are some positive signs that are beginning to develop. The MACD histogram has turned bullish for the past two days and the stochastics just had a bullish crossover. Therefore, hope is not completely lost for the bulls as the stock may be seeing some reprieve from the intense selling pressure.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the late January peak and the six-month low set just days ago, potential retracement resistance zones are identified above. The nearest level is 23.6% in the mid to high 160s. This area may have acted as a bit of resistance on the previous trading day and may continue, preventing further gains in the stock. If Broadcom is able to breakout from this level, the next challenge would be the 38.2% level, which is just above the round number 180.

Projection

Using the late January high again and the early March low with the retracement to around 200, the Fibonacci Projection shows potential support levels for the stock. Two trading sessions ago, Broadcom broke beneath the 61.8% level that was in the mid-150s, but it was able to recover. On the previous trading day, the stock seems to have found some support at this 61.8% level, as the candlestick was able to close above it. Do note that the next support level indicated here at the 100% level is below 130 and so other than the 61.8% level, support from the Fibonacci Projection is relatively far away.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock's recent plunge has seen it fall far beneath the cloud. This shows there is general bearishness currently in Broadcom. However, the cloud itself remains positive for the time being as the Leading Span A is still above Leading Span B until late April. Moving into May, the cloud may be a key source of resistance as the thickness continues to increase with the Leading Span A continuing to fall. In terms of the near term, the Conversion Line broke beneath the Base Line back in late February to show that momentum had shifted bearish. The gap between these two lines is significant currently, but with the Base Line's trajectory worse than the Conversion Line's, bearish momentum may actually be slowing in the near term. Lastly, the Lagging Span has, of course, rebounded along with the stock, but it's still far from late February levels in Broadcom. If the stock were to hold relatively steady from here, the gap between the Lagging Span and Broadcom's historic price would decrease into early March to show a decrease in bearish momentum.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

Broadcom reported their FY2025 Q1 earnings in early March and showed respectable results overall. The company posted revenues of $14.9 billion, up 25% YoY while adjusted EPS was $1.60, up 45% from the prior year period's $1.10. Their Infrastructure Software segment saw superior growth with revenues increasing 47% while their Semiconductor Solutions segment only saw growth of 11%. As a result, the contribution to revenues of their semiconductor business has shrunk to 55% versus the prior 62%. However, they reported that their AI semiconductor solutions saw 77% growth to total $4.1 billion within this segment, and so clearly the AI boom is continuing for the company. Both revenues and EPS were able to surpass expectations as they beat by $325.27 million and $0.09 respectively, showing outperformance relative to analyst estimates. As shown in the above chart, revenue growth has slowed from the prior quarter and is now back to only around average levels relative to the past few years. For cash flow from operations, they reported $6.1 billion, up 27% over the year ago quarter. As shown above, this represents high above-average growth that is only best by FY2022 Q4. Overall, I believe it is clear that Broadcom's fundamentals remained strong through their Q1 as growth was still healthy in key financial metrics.

Guidance

Broadcom provided brief guidance figures to present their outlook for FY2025 Q2. They are expecting to see revenues of approximately $14.9 billion which represents growth of 19% YoY. This continues the trend of growth slowdown from their Q1. Specifically for their AI semiconductors, they are projecting $4.4 billion in revenue for their Q2. With FY2024 Q2 AI revenues being $3.1 billion, this guidance is implying growth of 41.9% YoY. This is also a slowdown for their AI products, as they saw 77% growth in this figure for their Q1. Overall, a slowdown in growth was to be expected as the growth base continues to increase and so as a whole, I would still consider these guidance figure to be robust for the company. In addition, Seeking Alpha currently has a Revisions Grade of A for Broadcom as 97.22% of FY1 Revisions have been upward over the past 90 days. This shows that confidence for the outlook is highly strong at this point.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

The P/S ratio currently sits at 14.01 after significant contraction to start 2025. The multiple was significantly above 20 and is now slightly above the average level relative to the past few years. I believe this ratio shows around fair valuation to perhaps slight overvaluation for Broadcom stock. As I discussed earlier, for their Q1, the company saw their revenue growth dip back to around average levels. For their Q2, their guidance shows that growth will slow a bit more to 19% which seems to be a slightly below average level compared to prior years. The P/S ratio has contracted significantly to reflect this but remains a bit above its multiyear average, leading me to believe there is mild overvaluation in Broadcom stock. The current median P/S ratio for the Information Technology sector is only 2.36 but with Broadcom having a strong leadership position in highly-sought-after ASICs, I believe this premium is to be expected for such a dominant company.

Stock Buybacks

As reported by Seeking Alpha on April 7th, Broadcom announced a new share repurchase program that will run through December 31st, 2025. They are planning to buy back up to $10 billion of their own stock through both the open market and private negotiations. In my view, this is a clear message that the company is confident in their fundamentals and believe that now is a great opportunity to buy back their stock at an attractive valuation.

Takeaway

Overall, I believe Broadcom is around fairly valued, as the P/S ratio's contraction quite accurately reflects the results and guidance that the company provided in their latest earnings report. However, the announcement of the share repurchase program may indicate that management is highly confident over the direction of the business and that the stock may actually be cheap relative to growth prospects that are unknown to the public at this point. Therefore, I believe at the current valuation, Broadcom is still relatively attractive as the fundamentals may be stronger than previously thought.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the weak technicals and modestly bullish fundamentals lead me to believe that Broadcom is currently a hold. For the technicals, most indications are negative, and the few positives likely show a near term reprieve rather than a bullish reversal. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were respectable and guidance was overall satisfactory despite a growth slowdown being projected. The P/S ratio has contracted to reflect this but remains a bit higher than expected, potentially showing some overvaluation. As stated above, the company did just announce a buyback program, however, and so there are reasons to be bullish on the stock at this valuation. Overall, with this mixture of technical and fundamental signals, I believe a hold rating is most appropriate for Broadcom currently.

data.title: Pfizer: A Strategy For A Chaotic Implied Volatility
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) during the New Year, and I assigned it a “Hold” status. Although the stock seemed slightly undervalued, I was not thrilled about it due to high uncertainties related to their COVID franchise, drug prices, and a combination of multiple patent expirations in the coming years and a potentially rich yet not certain pipeline.

Due to a very low IV (implied volatility), I presented a dynamic hedging strategy with a long Put option and about 50 shares for a close to zero-delta position, with the number of shares increasing for a lower price and decreasing for a higher price, and the Put option sold on a higher IV (which is expected before earnings, for example). This position brought about 2.65% ROI in about one month (in a margin account). This strategy does not promise spectacular returns, but it is a low-risk strategy. For example, an average of ~4% / month (my average return in 2024) combined with a low risk is an interesting combination.

However, that strategy requires a very low IV and today we are in a completely different environment, with the market crashing and IV surging last week due to the tariff chaos:

Since in a low IV environment a strategy containing long options is interesting, in a high IV environment we can look for selling options, because we can get fat premiums.

Business Highlights

If we take a look at Pfizer’s top products for 2024, we can observe that the sales from their COVID vaccine, Comirnaty, dropped more than 50% vs. 2023 due to a decrease in vaccinations globally, confirming the very high uncertainty associated with this franchise:

More than $21B of revenue will be under pressure in the next five years due to patent expirations, and that if we exclude Prevnar 13 (because they have a new patent for Prevnar 20) and other lower sales products. That is about one third of 2024 revenue. Add to that the fluctuations from the COVID franchise (Paxlovid, but especially Comirnaty) with another $11B sales (about one sixth of total revenue), and some pressure on drug prices and we have a serious risk.

On the other side, let’s take a look at possible replacements (only recently approved or advanced stage products with >$100m potential peak sales):

Of course, the situation is a little blurred for some of these new drugs, but I think we can expect $12B - $14B peak sales. I also think that we can expect about $11B - $12B to be lost due to patent expirations (about 50% from the current sales). Adding some other possible losses for the Comirnaty vaccine or due to drug prices renegotiation, we can’t really expect growth for the next 5 years.

Financial Analysis

The company reiterated its 2025 financial guidance, for $2.8 - $3 non-GAP EPS:

But consensus EPS estimates for the next 5 years are not all rosy, predicting a small decline in EPS from $2.95 in 2025 to $2.7 in 2030. And considering the balance between patent expiration and pipeline discussed above, this is very close to what I think. We can observe that especially in 2028 and 2029 a ~6% EPS decline is expected, since there will be many patents expiring from 2026 to 2028:

Pfizer’s outlined capital allocation strategy is:

  • Maintaining and growing the dividend over time: in my initial article I worked with a dividend growth rate of ~2.3% for the next five years, but now I think that might be a bit too high. 1% seems more appropriate, in 2030 they would have about 67% payout ratio, a bit elevated but plausible, since most patent expirations will have occurred by 2028.
  • Reinvesting in the business at an appropriate level of financial return: I don’t think that's the case for now, they still have to digest the acquisition of Seagen.
  • Making value-enhancing share repurchases after delevering the balance sheet: gross leverage target of 3.25x by the end of 2025 is a first priority.

Fair Value

I don’t think that a comparison with Pfizer’s five-year averages is reliable, due to bloated multiples in the COVID years, but a comparison with peers might be useful, since the healthcare sector is less affected by tariffs and recessions.

I also presented in my initial article two fair value estimates based on proprietary DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) models from Morningstar and from Finbox, but I don’t agree with those estimates (at least not today). For example, I think they use a too low WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital) for a company not shy about uncertainties. I generally use my own DCF model, but in this case, a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) is a very good approximation.

Tool #1: Comparison with peers

The most useful multiple (in this case) for which I want to make this comparison is EV/EBITDA because it includes profitability and debt:

With an EV/EBITDA FWD = 6.62, Pfizer looks about 23% undervalued compared with the selected group of peers, which would translate into a FV = $26.25.

Tool #2: DDM (base case scenario)

My assumptions are:

  • 1% dividend growth in the next five years, for about 67% payout ratio in 2030.
  • 2% growth in perpetuity, most patent expirations will have occurred by 2028.
  • 8.4% discount rate, a little higher than in my initial article: 4.15% risk-free rate, 5% equity risk and a 0.85 adjusted Beta.

Fair Value is $26.25, about the same 24% potential upside from the current price ($21.2).

Risk scenario:

Let’s suppose that an unfortunate combination will happen in about one or two years: drug prices and COVID sales will be dragged down due to political pressures (there are also other political accusations more recently), maybe one of their new products in an advanced phase will be delayed or discontinued. In this situation, they might miss the lower end of their EPS range target. Combining with the necessity to further delever, they might want to slash the dividend.

For example, with $2.7 EPS in 2025 and looming patent losses, suppose they will slash the dividend to $1.15, about 42% payout ratio. From there, they could increase by $0.05 per year, or around 4% until 2030. With the same 2% growth in perpetuity and 8.4% discount rate, Fair Value would be $19.28.

Another risk is related to new tariffs on pharmaceuticals and, of course, the follow-up with retaliation, escalation etc. Pfizer does not seem much affected by that, with manufacturing locations around the world, so I would not take that into consideration for the Fair Value. However, short-term stock prices might be dragged down by such events along with the entire pharma industry.

Differences Between a Covered Call and a Short Put

Like mentioned in the introduction, we are today in a chaotic high IV environment. The VIX index was this week at 59, a value exceeded only in 2020 spring (remember that time?) or in 2008 autumn (remember that time?).

In this environment, we can try to profit from selling options. Two such simple and very related strategies are covered Call (selling a Call option per 100 shares) and short Put (selling a Put option). There are, however, some differences.

Distinction #1: Interest rates

When interest rates are (very) high, Call options are more expensive than Put options. That’s why I wrote several articles focusing on selling Call options in the last year. From this point of view, a covered Call would be slightly more interesting than a short Put.

Distinction #2: Dividend

With a covered Call strategy, we are entitled to a dividend for 100 shares, but with a short Put we are not. However, Put prices are higher for high-dividend stocks like Pfizer, the market factoring in a price readjustment on the ex-dividend date. This is especially true for ITM (In-The-Money) and ATM (At-The-Money) Put options.

Distinction #3: Account type

There are two types of accounts:

  • “Cash” account: when opening a short Put position, this should be fully covered by cash. For example, with a short PFE 21 Put, I need $2100 in my portfolio (after receiving the premium). This might be useful as a margin of safety for investors who would be satisfied to own the stock if assigned, otherwise the return is only a little higher than for a simple stock position.
  • “Margin” account: the required margin fluctuates with the underlying stock price; with the stock fluctuating around the strike price, the margin is somewhere between 10% and 20% of the strike price. Let’s be more prudent and let’s take 30%, leaving more room for a decrease; for a PFE 21 Put that means about $630 required, much less than for a “cash” account.

What if the stock will go even lower than that 30% protection in a margin account? First of all, I sell a short Put only for slightly undervalued stocks and when IV is very high and hoping that will decrease, so that could drag down the Put price. Then, I only use this strategy for stocks less affected by tariffs or recessions, like healthcare or regulated utilities, otherwise I would use a Put Spread. Finally, I always have a “buffer” cash & equivalents position for rainy days (covering my entire portfolio), and it happens that my buffer is way overfull after selling last year many rallying positions and after derisking my portfolio during the last month, when signs of the storm were already visible.

Strategy: I sold today A PFE 21 Put expiring November’25 for $247. That means a break-even at $18.53, and I like that because it comes just a bit under my “risk” scenario fair value. Although the margin is a little lower today, I consider a cost of 30%, or $630 (leaving more room for a decrease as described above). Here is what we could have at expiration in different scenarios:

But as always, I might close the position (much) sooner. For example, if IV will plummet and/or stock price will go up and my Put would value less than $100 in, say, one or two months, I see no reason to take the risk to keep it until November, and the annualized ROI would be (much) higher.

Takeaway

Pfizer faces significant revenue pressure from patent expirations and declining COVID vaccine sales. Potential replacements from the pipeline are estimated to bring at most flat growth prospects over the next five years.

A base case Fair value estimate indicates a potential 23% upside, based on a DDM with 1% growth for the next five years, 2% growth in perpetuity and 8.4% discount rate, and backed by a comparison with peers. Risks include further political pressure on drug prices and vaccines.

My dynamic hedging strategy proposed at the beginning of January, with a close to zero-delta position made of shares and long Put options, yielded a 2.65% ROI in about one month in a low IV environment.

However, current extreme IV suggests selling options for fat premiums. Selling an ATM Put option expiring Nov'25 could bring up to 39% ROI at expiration, or about 65% annualized, or even higher if the option is closed early.

data.title: AXA: Let The Ride Begin
data.description:
Following our neutral rating on Allianz driven by three key points (1. valuation with near peak P/E, 2. lower remuneration compared to peers, and 3. EBIT Profit 3 years CAGR already priced in and aligned with peers average growth of 6%), we are back to comment on AXA SA (OTCQX:AXAHY, OTCQX:AXAHF). Last year, we reported our comment on AXA Capital Market Day (Unlock The Future, Buy Rating Confirmed). Then, we provided an optimistic view following the French election disruption (Short-Term Volatility, Long-Term Gain). As a reminder, our buy rating was backed by AXA's scale, combined ratio evolution, and a best-in-class remuneration story. Following the double-digit stock price decline in the last 5 days (-11%), we reiterate our Buy rating on AXA.

Fig 1

Why are we still supportive?

  1. In Fiscal Year 2024, the company delivered an 8% year-on-year growth in earnings per share, in line with the top end of its 6-8% 3-year target (Fig 2). In number, AXA recorded underlying earnings of €8.1 billion with P&C earnings of €5.5 billion (2% ahead of Wall Street consensus estimates). This was driven by a combined ratio coming in at 91.0%. According to our analysis, this output was due to 60 basis points lower Nat Cats and 20 basis points of higher discount. Also, the loss ratio has improved. Therefore, this is a good starting point for the Unlock the Future implementation business plan. On a positive note, P&C prices remain extremely favorable in SMEs across geography. We should report that half of the plan's P&C improvement journey has been achieved in year 1. Therefore, we see several avenues in which AXA could be positively surprised in the upcoming years. For this reason, the company aims to ensure supportive results across the cycle and expects to maintain margins through the efficiency gains. This will be evident in L&H's 2025 earnings, with growth opportunities in the near term. Additionally, AXA business volumes and improved persistency continue to grow over time. Compared to peers, AXA has a higher operating profit growth (8% vs. an average of 6%). And our new 2025-2026 EPS estimates factor in an 8% year-on-year growth. In late December 2024, the company decided to deconsolidate its asset management business from H2 2025. This follows the expected sale of AXA Investment Managers to BNP Paribas. This was a lower performing division in French insurance; for this reason, we see AXA's new plan as achievable;

  2. Even in this challenging market momentum, AXA could benefit from its solid balance sheets and supportive Solvency II ratio. Regarding the fiscal year number, we report a ratio of 216% (Fig 2), which meets consensus expectations. On a positive note, the company reported a net loss of €100 million in the California wildfires, which was below our expectations. AXA reiterated a 4.5 basis points natural catastrophe budget, which confirmed its earnings resiliency. As a reminder, we favor multi-lines insurance within the financials sector;

  3. AXA’s major capital return cycle will progress well with the recent announcement of the €1.2 billion buyback program. Until the summer of 2026, considering the current dividend yield, we see 20% of the market capitalisation returned to shareholders. Currently, the company has one of the most attractive total return yields within the insurance sector, especially amongst the large multi-line insurers. This also takes into account a payout ratio of 75% (Fig 3). Compared to Allianz, which is progressing with new investments in the emerging markets, and Generali's potential merger, the AXA new capital management framework removes M&A risk in our base case scenario.

Source: AXA FY 2024 results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

Following the Fiscal Year 2024 results, we reviewed upward AXA estimates. The first year of AXA’s new strategic cycle was strong, and we see many positive trends ahead, continuing to think that AXA can grow more than 6-8% EPS over the plan period. We have moved our buyback from €400 million to €1.2 billion compared to our previous indication. Aside from supportive shareholders' remuneration (above peers), we continue to see several avenues AXA could positively surprise in coming years, such as better L&H earnings and resilient commercial pricing in the P&C division. This was evident in the personal lines pricing of +10% in Q4, most notably in Ireland and the UK. With a divestment of the Investment Manager division, we slightly increased our EBIT projection to €8.3 billion, with a 2% increase in our 2025 EPS. Rolling forward our valuation and arriving at an EPS of €3.9, we increased our valuation. As a reminder, we used to value multi-line insurance at a target P/E of 10x. Currently, AXA trades at 8.4x. Therefore, we value the company with a target price of €39 per share based on a 10x P/E. This is below peers' average (9.64x - Fig 5) and its historical valuation (9.01x - Fig 5).

Fig 5

Risks

We have long coverage of AXA risks, with a 10-year analysis of its main financials ratio. So, we report a wide variety of risks, including 1) equity market volatility related to reinvestment yield performance, asset management division lower performance and sensitivity to the Solvency II ratio, 2) tax and regulatory changes, 3) debtor default risk, 4) higher Nat Cat loss, 5) change in the company's real assets portfolio, and 6) FX fluctuation (related to earnings and private/public investment changes in value).

Conclusion

AXA's FY 2024 performance was solid, showing a strong position to deliver on the three-year plan. Our core investment thesis is around EPS growth and a solid capital return. For this reason, our buy rating is confirmed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Franklin Covey: Post-Selloff Thoughts
data.description:
Introduction

Franklin Covey’s (NYSE:FC) stock has seen its rise and fall, growing from ~$18 in 2020 to $50+ in late 2022 to then declining to ~$38 in early 2025. After Q2 2025 earnings were released, however, shares have now tumbled to ~$20 today, practically erasing any gain on a 5-year basis.

Simply put, recent demand declines have resulted in a permanent loss of capital on my end. While I don’t necessarily believe remaining invested at FC in the upper-$30/share territory was a mistake, recent events have realized the bear case of the outcome distribution which has objectively reduced their intrinsic value and thus, my returns.

But with that now a sunk cost, the focus is back to looking ahead, not dwelling on the past. To this end, at the risk of thesis drift, I also think it’s objectively true that there’s nothing wrong structurally with FC. Results have degraded, sure, but for macro reasons, not competitive ones. In other words, albeit materially, I think the story has just been pushed to the right, offering opportunistic investors an attractive buying opportunity today.

Enterprise: Macro Issues

Q2 Enterprise sales were $43.6M, down ~4.5% from $45.6M posted in last year’s Q2. Broken out further, this was mostly stemming from a reduction in AAP revenue, both subscription and service revenue, with a smaller portion coming from a decline in legacy revenue. International licensee revenue was about flat.

If you read my Q1 earnings write-up, I spill out what I think has been happening, which is very simply that year-over-year results have been negatively impacted by a combination of (1) macro-related pressures resulting in clients deferring expansions or upgrades to some materially reducing their spend or cancelling contracts entirely, and (2) a reduction in CPs (client partners, or sales reps) - mostly point 1 though. I do not believe they have been losing any market share.

With Q2 now released, the directional mechanics and my conclusions are unchanged, but the magnitude needs to be adjusted in my model as current demand has softened further with incremental pressures on future demand. Let’s just get straight down to the news - the market sold FC off because of AAP softness, which primarily stemmed from their government exposure. Here’s the reality summed up on the call:

“While we feel good about progress across the vast majority of our business, we are experiencing direct and indirect impact from the actions being taken by the federal government and by the resultant economic uncertainty. …

Roughly 6%, or $17 million of our total business is in some way tied to governmental entities such as the Department of Defense, Veterans Affairs and state and local entities. Approximately $5 million in government revenue has already been canceled or postponed as the federal government looks to cut spending in nearly every category. …

The second area in which we're seeing some impact from these same government actions and trade tensions is in our international direct and licensee operations. A significant portion of this impact is being felt in China, where the threat of escalating tariffs and other potential factors and political factors is causing a sensitivity by national and even local companies in China to being viewed as doing business with U.S. firms.

In a small number of cases, we've also had clients in Europe and Canada postpone or cancel engagements either as a defensive or nationalistic response to the threat, or the reality of tariff actions, or as a response to the uncertainty of the economic conditions in their own country. ...”

With this in mind, here’s what I still believe about FC:

  • They have a durable ROI. I do not believe today’s sales decline is a reflection of a deterioration in the value proposition or market share loss, but simply macro-driven declines. If such declines were attributable to the value proposition or share-driven, that would be inconsistent on a number of levels, such as their history, retention rates, the qualitative context of the offering today, and their strategy.

  • They can grow their business. Per the conclusion above about their ROI and the fact that, as stated in prior write-ups, they have a long runway ahead of them, their strategy to grow their sales reps is a reasonable one and will generate a large amount of incremental sales. There’s a number of clients that remain untapped in earnest.

So, let’s adjust the model for the changes. With the governmental losses partially now in the numbers, today’s sales now amount to about $190M annually on a run rate basis, bringing down the model a material amount already. Going forward, I think there are 2 directional forces at play.

  1. The macro and tariff situation will worsen and reduce sales. Cost-wise, FC isn’t going to incur much impact given their asset-light model and costs being primarily people, so pricing will largely go unchanged. However, they’ll incur indirect impacts - as demand at some of their clients worsens, there’s a good chance some customers pull back on spend in the next 12 months, just as some have in the past 12 months. And not only from governmental clients, but enterprises too. And to this point too, their international exposure - ~15% of sales - is subject to further declines as geopolitical tensions rise as evident today.

  2. As an offsetting structural trend, I believe they’re going to add more reps and grow the business, even if they don’t do it at the pace originally planned. Recall that before the macro softness, they estimated that they could add ~$120M in consolidated sales from the end of FY25 until the end of FY28, all of which was Enterprise growth practically speaking, implying double-digit growth annually. Is that still achievable? I think so. It doesn’t sound at the moment at least that they’ve curtailed their hiring plans. However, I’m assuming some pushes out a few years, implying that perhaps this is more achievable by FY30 or FY31. Regardless, however, I do think they can largely capture this level of sales growth - let’s just say, $100M.

Tariffs-aside, today’s macro isn’t abnormal - i.e., maybe they decline further in the interim, it’s reasonable to me that they’ll eventually recover back to this level. Now, tariffs will structurally reduce this. How much? I don’t know. Maybe you knock about $2M from International sales, and then maybe 5% from their U.S. sales? In 2018, sales were minimally impacted as a reference point. But that would imply sales of ~$40M in Q4, or ~$175M annually. Seems aggressive, particularly against their guide and particularly if tariffs are dialed back.

From that base though, if they capture $100M in incremental sales over the next ~7 years from CP growth then grow sales 3.5% for the next half decade thereafter via price and residual volume growth, that implies FY35 sales of ~$327M.

To round this out, my thoughts really haven’t changed with respect to future EBITDA margins, as the mechanics really haven’t changed in Q2. Previously, I was thinking they could do 25% segment EBITDA margins on $280M in sales, but as I’m lengthening my model and assuming they grow to ~$327M by FY35, I deem high-20% margins - say, 28% - reasonable.

As a referenceable base, they were producing mid-20% segment EBITDA margins on FY24 sales of ~$210M - i.e., before any hiring ramp materially impacting CP productivity. Knock off about $10M in annual sales attributable to government and ascribe a 50% decremental to that, that would still imply low-to-mid-20% EBITDA margins. By FY35, however, we should see a similar CP productivity mix at that point plus additional fixed cost leverage, for which ~30% incrementals are certainly reasonably achievable.

So, I’m modeling 28% margins on $327M in FY35 sales, or EBITDA of ~$92M. Note though that in the interim, margins may decline faster as sales decline before costs are rationalized.

Valuation

Before jumping into the model, they are anticipating some softness for their Education business, which grew just marginally year-over-year to just over $24M in the quarter. Long story short, while they’ve seen fine demand lately, the federal government spending reduction is likely to reduce school funding, implicitly reducing spending at some of FC’s prospective schools. Education sales are about $75M annually on a run rate basis - minus another, say, 7%, and I get to ~$70M. They can grow from there. However, I’m simply keeping sales flat over time at 11% margins (~$8M EBITDA).

My FY35 model then contains consolidated sales/EBITDA of $401M/$99M ($324M/$92M for Enterprise and the above for Education). Minus $18M of unallocated corporate expense, D&A of ~$9M (2.25% of sales), SBC of ~$17M (4.25% of sales), zero interest expense, 25% tax rate, and capex of ~$17M (4.25% of sales), that implies FCF of $47M.

At ~$20/share, they’re trading at a ~$263M market cap. Thus, if they grow low-to-mid-single-digits over time beyond FY29 - which I find achievable - that’s an attractive ~12-13% IRR. Furthermore, this doesn’t include any additional benefit from capital allocation, for which they’re likely going to repurchase a large number of shares - this could add 100 to 200 bps to the IRR.

The biggest risk is merely misexecution or misjudgment of the opportunity. Yes, the macro could push out economic realization and bring down the IRR, but the future is largely predicated on the growth opportunity. But given their track record of growth plus qualitative reasonability for growth, I’m okay making this bet.

However, it is interesting that, if you make the assumption that they can’t grow which would thus imply management pulling back on hiring to push margins back to historical levels, you’re left with about a $20M in FCF business per annum. At low-single-digit growth, that’s worth about $250M, or about where the stock is trading today.

Conclusion

Perhaps I’m mistakenly attributing the recent demand softness to macro headwinds, but per my inference of the data plus go-forward strategy, the future economic reality I think is likely achievable supports a higher price.

data.title: GDXJ: A Perfect Storm For Gold Miners
data.description:
Gold miners have a perfect storm for earnings growth in 2025. I previously covered the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) for Seeking Alpha back in October. In that piece, my view was that any dips in the mining space were buy opportunities. This is a sentiment that I shared in coverage of the Sprott Gold Miners ETF (SGDM) as well in December and March. As expected, we've had a dip in the miners in recent months, yet prices have quickly recovered.

Not only have we seen the Gold miners rally strongly from their Q4-24 declines, but the sector is now substantially ahead of the broader equity market. I can summarize my overall thesis on the mining space pretty simply; energy down, Gold up is a terrific setup for miners because the value output is improving while their cost input should decline, all else (production capacity) being equal.

For a mining industry that has had a difficult time rallying above COVID-era levels even as Gold broke out well above $2,000/oz in 2024, I think the chart above is a compelling illustration of how good the setup is for Gold miners should be right now. To sort of hammer this home, we can do a normalized percentage gain over the last ten years for the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), the price of diesel 2, and the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDXJ):

This shows how well GDXJ has typically performed when Gold prices rise while energy cost goes down. In the last two bull cycles for miners, those percentage gains have far surpassed the gains seen from simply holding the metal itself. While they're certainly waking up, we have yet to see the miners - particularly the juniors - put up gains in excess of the metal as their margins improve. I suspect we'll see funds like GDXJ perform the way they have previously.

GDXJ Details and Holdings

I've already made the case for both GDX and SGDM in prior Seeking Alpha articles. I still hold both of them. SGDM for active management and GDX for the more broad exposure to the mining sector. In my opinion, GDXJ is worth a look as well because it focuses on the smaller equities in the sector and offers exposure to exploration stage companies.

Fund Details GDXJ GDX SGDM
Issuer VanEck VanEck Sprott
Inception 11/10/2009 05/16/2006 07/14/2014
Expense Ratio 0.52% 0.51% 0.50%
AUM $5.31B $14.99B $330.10M
Number of Holdings 92 62 37
Assets in Top 10 41.91% 64.17% 66.31%
Turnover 28.00% 15.00% 81.00%

Source: Seeking Alpha

From a cost standpoint, GDXJ is essentially the same as both GDX and SGDM, where it's a bit different is in the holdings. At 92 positions, GDXJ is casting the largest net of the three funds and has the lowest allocation to top 10 holdings. While turnover is almost double GDX at 28%, GDXJ isn't actively managed to the degree that SGDM is.

What you'll notice from the holdings table is top miners like Barrick Gold Corporation (GOLD), Newmont Corporation (NEM), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) and Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) are not in the fund at all. Again, this is by design, as GDXJ is tailored to investors who want exposure to the smaller companies in the space due to what is believed to be better upside potential in a strong bull market.

Can This Setup Continue?

The obvious question; can this setup for miners continue? I believe it can, and I hold that view because I think Gold is moving on a very serious macro shift away from the dollar and US treasuries. Here are some signals to consider:

Yields on longer duration US bonds would have figured to go down when the equity market faced sell pressure from tariffs as investors sought 'safety.' That has not happened. The yields on the 7, 10, 20, and 30 year bonds are all higher than where they were one month ago and six months ago. At least some of this is likely due to foreign nations selling US debt, either to protect their own currencies or punitively because of potential US tariffs. Either way, Gold is sending an important message as well. Even with US equities and credit both under pressure, the metal is ripping hard. I believe this shows a sustained buyer that is insensitive to price.

Furthermore, energy prices may remain low due to a potential 2025 recession. JPMorgan (JPM) has recession odds at 60%. Polymarket speculators have the odds slightly higher. Though I'll note the US recession in 2025 futures market through that platform has substantially less trading volume than what we observed in the lead up to the November election. A real risk to my long Gold miners idea would be the mining stocks coming down with broad market equities in a recession:

Adding the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) to our ten year GDXJ and Gold trading history shows GDXJ generally spikes explosively from broad market bottoms. I don't think we've put in a broad market bottom yet in equities. So, it is possible that Gold miners decline in sympathy with US stocks rather than increase as Gold approaches $3,500/oz.

However, I'm not totally convinced that pressure on the index will bring down Gold miners with it because there isn't much Gold mining representation in the index to begin with. Newmont is in the S&P 500, but the juniors aren't. Thus, I think even in the event Newmont faces prices pressure due to declines in the S&P 500, GDXJ should have some immunity from that.

Closing Takeaways

I'll reiterate that I think this is a pretty simple idea; energy down with Gold up is a strong setup for Gold mining stocks. For broad exposure to the sector, it's difficult to argue against simply buying GDX or a fund like the iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (RING). For those who want more active management, I think SGDM remains a solid choice. Of course, there's a product for everyone, and Gold miners are no different. For those with a higher threshold for potential pain, GDXJ might make some sense as well. The fund has exposure to the juniors and even to companies that are not yet producing. But with higher metal prices and energy costs down, projects that aren't currently online might look a lot more economical in 2025 and beyond. I like GDXJ as well and will initiate coverage with a 'buy.'

data.title: Nintendo: Video Gaming Dominance And Underpricing Should Support Its Rebound After The Pullback
data.description:
A quarter after my first coverage of Nintendo Co, Ltd. (OTCPK:NTDOY)(OTCPK:NTDOF)(NEOE:NTDO:CA), I can say my buy rating is still applicable due to its capacity to increase its market reach and stay competitive. Its solid performance and excellent Balance Sheet are some things that will sustain its expansion and evolution in the following years. These can even justify its upside despite the evident technical risks. At this point, investors may wonder if it’s a perfect time to buy this stock as selling pressures increase. We will discuss these aspects one by one.

Q3/9M Performance

We still see the decreasing YoY trend in NTDOY’s income statement figures. Its net operating revenue during Q3 amounted to ¥432.9B, down by -27% YoY from ¥598.6B. This was still a strong performance considering that it has already been eight years since the release of the original Nintendo Switch. This means that many players are still patronizing this hybrid video game console. In fact, the number of annual playing users still increased from my previous coverage at 127M to 129M. That’s still a 2% increase, which shows that more people are joining our community and that the Switch has not lost its popularity.

It is also worth noting that it performed better in Q3 than in the other two quarters of FY 2025. In fact, its Q3 revenue was a solid rebound from Q1 and Q2 with only ¥246.6B and ¥276.7B. Hence, we can say that Nintendo still enjoys an increasing demand for consoles and games and not just mere seasonality as shown in the past eleven quarters. Another important aspect is pricing strategy and geographical diversification with increased international market presence as its sales outside Japan comprise 76.5% of the total amount.

What I like most about its most recent performance is its operational efficiency. Its operating costs remained manageable despite the sticky inflation in the US, Japan, and EU from October to January. We can see that the proportion of Nintendo’s operating costs to the total costs and expenses dropped from 70.4% to 60.4% YoY. This shows a lower variable cost contribution, which, I think, is impressive. NTDOY had a lower cost sensitivity despite the sudden inflation uptick, proving it knows how to handle its operations very well and balance growth with profitability. As a result, its gross profit margin improved a lot from 51.2% to 57.1% YoY. Meanwhile, the operating margin remained relatively flat at 30%. In addition, it was much higher than in Q1 and Q2 at 22% and 24%, showing improved management.

Nintendo Main Drivers

These are the things I think will sustain Nintendo’s capacity and drive its growth amid macroeconomic uncertainties.

Balance Sheet Is Still One Of Its Core Strengths

Nintendo has maintained stable and adequate cash reserves over the years. In the past 11 quarters, it has already increased by 28.8% to ¥1.4T. Its short-term investments have already increased substantially, keeping its capital buffer high. Their combined amount comprises more than half of its assets and can cover all liabilities, showing its excess liquidity. I think that’s normal since it has low capital intensity. Another thing I like about NTDOY is that it keeps its borrowings zero. This shows that it can sustain its capacity and continue creating new games and consoles without borrowing. That is a plus point, especially now that interest rates across different countries are still elevated.

Nintendo’s Capitalization On Gaming Experience Continues To Pay Off

Over the years, Nintendo has learned what would work, that is to not turn back. One thing I like about Nintendo is the fact that it has never stopped evolving and capitalizing on new digital trends. Its high adaptability is what continues to propel its growth from Japan to Western countries. The Switch, for instance, can be played anytime, and anywhere you want, whether you're riding a bus or staying at home with your family. The feature that allows multiple players at once not only makes it a gaming device but also a way to connect family members and friends.

With the Switch 2, the fun continues. Nintendo retains backward compatibility, allowing people to play the games they enjoyed from the Switch 1. Although we, as avid players, haven’t confirmed their differences, I believe the Switch 2 will offer improved performance, such as better and larger screen display, graphics, joy-con design, battery life, and storage.

Risks and Mitigants

These are some aspects we must know.

Tariffs Versus Oil

Nintendo Switch 2 is coming into a much more challenging market landscape than its predecessor did eight years ago. It is about to be launched amid President Trump’s tariffs, which primarily target Asian countries, a big source of tech supply chains. Japan may face an additional 24% tariffs on its exports. This can affect the movement of its parts, which can become inflationary in the long run. The problem is the announced price of $449 considered old tariffs and how and where they were imposed. Hence, it may have to reassess its pricing strategy or find ways to improve cost efficiency since this will affect the demand, sales, and margins.

On a lighter note, there are some sprinkles of hope that can partially offset it. One is OPEC’s plan to increase its oil output by 138,000 and 135,000 barrels per day or bpd in the next two months. Increasing supply can lower oil prices this year. This means transporting and shipping products and parts may become cheaper once fuel prices drop. Lower oil prices can also affect other industries, such as manufacturing, restaurants, and automotive. As a result, this can temper inflation or at least improve the cost management of many businesses to stabilize their profits. This can also increase their production, income, and employment, improving the purchasing power of consumers. Hence, this can partially counter tariffs, allowing Nintendo to manage costs or set more strategic pricing.

Competitive Environment Versus The Gap Nintendo Will Bridge

Aside from macroeconomic changes, Nintendo Switch 2 is facing a more competitive environment than the Switch 1 did before. Examples include The Steam Deck by the Valve. It was launched in 2022 as a response to the Nintendo Switch for computer gamers. This enabled players to access their computer games like Dota and Team Fortress using a handheld gaming device. Meanwhile, Sony’s PlayStation Portal came out in 2023 to let gamers play even without connecting it to a television. Microsoft is now conceptualizing an idea to let any device become an Xbox by accessing its games from its cloud service. Also, the Switch is more expensive than the PS 5 at $400, the Xbox Series X at $450, and the Steam Deck at $399.

Despite this, these competitors are still far behind Nintendo. Valve just shipped nearly 6M units compared to Nintendo with over 100M Switches. There are still a lot of gaps that the Switch 2 may bridge. Thanks to its powerful hardware, which boasts higher storage, enhanced screen display, 4k support, and a better cooling system. In addition, its new Joy-Con controllers can act like a computer mouse, which shows that Nintendo can integrate video games with the computer gaming crowd. So, I believe Nintendo is still ahead of this race.

DCF Model

At this point, I have already made a lot of adjustments to my previous DCF Model. The TP decreased from my coverage at ¥19,231 to ¥18,432. This is a -4% change, which, I think, is logical for several reasons.

In my previous coverage, I just derived the revenue by simply getting the average revenue growth. However, this time, I believe that using CAGR would not reflect the real value of the revenue. First, we have to understand that Nintendo derives its revenue mainly from Switch and games. As we can see, annual playing users YoY growth has decreased in recent years. In addition, its operating revenue has substantially decreased YoY throughout the whole FY 2025. For a more conservative approach, I just simply got the average revenue YoY growth/decrease of the three quarters to derive Q4. So, my projected revenue for FY 2025 is ¥1.2T, a -28.3% YoY decrease.

For FY 2026 to FY 2029, I applied the CAGR to project the operating revenue as an initial step. Then, I added the projected sales of Switch 2. For 2026, analysts project that Nintendo will sell 15-17M units for $450 each. The trend will continue until it sells 80M units. To be conservative, I estimated NTDOY to only sell 15M due to tariffs and competition. Then, I distributed the remaining 65M to the succeeding years. I also made a 10% allowance to account for the impact of tariffs. For instance, if 20M units will be sold in FY 2027, I would lower it to 18M.

For COGS and Operating Expenses, I just set them as a percentage of revenue. I did the same to the net working capital, except for accounts receivables, inventory, and accounts payable. I used the DSO, DIO, and DPO for them. Lastly, I estimated capex and depreciation using a fixed asset schedule.

For its perpetual growth rate, I set it at 2%. You may think it’s still too low, but I believe this will be a better rate to account for the risks, such as tariffs and competition. And as for the WACC, I derived it using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. Computing is easy because NTDOY has no borrowings. So, it’s the same as the cost of equity.

Technicals

NTDOY’s momentum has been weakening recently as negative market sentiments continue. The price has already crossed below the 50-day SMA.

Despite this, it is still trading above the 200-day SMA. We must also note the bullish rejection last April 7, showing traders did not accept any price lower than its opening price. In addition, the bullish rejection appeared just above the January 21 support. Although it already created a lower high and low, buying opportunities are still present. The current downtrend may also be attributed to a cautious approach of traders amid macroeconomic uncertainties.

Moreover, we can also notice that traders behave almost consistently with the Fibonacci Retracements. Buying and selling pressures increased when the price hit any of 0.50, 0.618, or 0.786 points. I also believe that the 0.50 point can dictate a reversal or a rebound after a pullback. Last December NTDOY consolidated at this level before a bullish breakout. This week, the bullish rejection began when the price hit this point again. At this level, many traders found it as a new entry point, pushing the price up by 8-10% the next day.

It is also worth noting that its RSI is already close to the oversold region, so buying opportunities are present. As of now, the trend of MACD and RSI remains consistent with the price, so we cannot detect any bearish reversal pattern. Despite this, I advise traders to do range trading as the market tests the support at the 0.50 retracement.

For our trading strategy using a 1:1 Risk Reward Ratio, take profit at ¥10,450, which is just below the 0.786 retracement point. I think this will be a spot as the volume weakens and reverses every time the price hits any of the three retracement points. Stop loss at ¥8,340, this is already at the 0.382 retracement point, which suggests that the selling volume intensifies. The price may test this as a support, and if it fails to hit the 0.50 point, it may retrace to the 0.236 point before hitting the 52-week low.

Conclusion

Nintendo Co. Ltd. remains well-positioned amid macroeconomic volatility and increased competition. Its efficiency, high liquidity, and continued evolution allow it to secure a solid customer base and sustain its expansion. In addition, it is still underpriced with attractive returns. Despite this, I advise investors to be more cautious as the market tests new support. I am downgrading it from strong buy to buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Verizon: Tariff Panic Offers Attractive Setup For An Option Play
data.description:
VZ stock: implied volatility hovers near 52-week high

My last analysis on Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ)(NEOE:VZ:CA) was published more than a month ago. That article was titled “Verizon Q4: Let Your Profit Run”. As the title hinted, the article served as a review for its Q4 earnings report (ER) and gave the stock a buy rating. Quote:

Since my last writing, Verizon has reported strong Q4 earnings. But its stock price advanced even more, resulting in a P/E expansion to 9.09x (from 8.5x at the time of my last writing). A reexamination of the stock shows that the current valuation is still attractive, with a risk premium at a multi-year low. Furthermore, VZ's dividend yield of 6.38% is compelling, with a payout ratio of 59.45%, leaving ample room for future growth and downside protection.

Since that writing, it feels like an understatement to say that there have been some crucial developments both in terms of macroeconomics and geopolitics as a result of the tariff conflicts. Against this macroscopic backdrop, the thesis of this article is to explain why I believe the option market is overestimating the uncertainties facing VZ. As a result, I see an attractive setup for investors to sell options for at least two reasons.

First, the implied volatilities (IV) underlying VZ options are near their 52-week peak level as you can see from the following screenshot taken from MarketChameleon. To wit, the table shows that the current IV of VZ is about 31.4, placing it in the 98% percentile rank in the past 52 weeks. In other words, its IV was lower than the current level 98% of the time in the past year. Moreover, the current IV of 31.4 is also substantially above its 20-day HV (aka historical or realized volatility) of 25.8 by more about 22%. Such an extreme IV makes pricing very favorable for option sellers (and I will revisit this later).

Second, I can see why the ongoing tariff situation poses extreme uncertainties for the overall market. But I think such an extreme volatility, as reflected in VZ’s current IV, is misplaced in the case of VZ. In the next section, I will analyze VZ’s profit exposure and argue why the stock is far less sensitive to higher tariffs rates than the overall economy.

VZ stock: profit exposure

I see a few key factors that make VZ stock far less sensitive to higher tariff rates than the overall economy. To start with, the company is primarily a provider of telecommunication services within the United States. Unlike many sectors heavily reliant on international trade and global supply chains (like manufacturing or retail), 100% of Verizon's revenue is domestic in recent years as you can see from the following chart.

Of course, the fact that its revenues are 100% generated domestically does not mean it is completely immune to higher tariffs. The company still needs to purchase equipment (some of which could be imported) and thus could be indirectly impacted by tariff changes. That is why we need to look further into the breakdown of its profit composition. The bar chart shown below serves this purpose by illustrating Verizon’s revenue exposure across various segments. As seen, the largest portion of revenue (more than a half at 57.04%) is derived from Consumer Service. The segment that is most likely to feel some impact from high tariffs is its Consumer Wireless Equipment in my model. But this segment contributes a relatively minor 14.54% to the total revenue only.

VZ stock: specific option ideas

Due to this above gap between the extreme IV and its relatively low sensitivity to tariffs, I think the current option premiums are attractively priced for sellers. To wit, the following option chain shows some specific options ideas for VZ (both put and call options) with an expiration date of July 18, 2025. I did not randomly pick this date. It is bit more than 90 days from now, the grace period that the U.S. has just granted to its trading partners (except China). Thus, options with this expiry could help sellers to maximize the benefits from the extreme volatility during this period.

Now, let’s look at a particular example, an NTM put option (near-the-money) with a strike of $44. This option last traded at a price of $2.45 as seen. With the stock price as of this writing ($43.6), this premium translates into a return of more than 5.6% in about 90 days. Thus, the extreme IV could help put sellers to either get the shares at lower effective prices or earn a pretty attractive premium income (more than 20% when annualized). Similarly, existing shareholder can consider the use of covered-call options to significantly boost the income on their VZ shares. Assuming the options are not exercised before expiry, call sellers can keep both the option premium and also collect the dividend payouts in the meantime (VZ currently offers a FWD yield of 6.3% as of this writing).

Other risks and final thoughts

Before concluding, let me point out some risks - both in the positive and also negative directions - entailed by my forgoing analysis. Before anything else, a strong caution is in order for investors new to options. Trading options are more complicated than trading shares directly, and the loss can be unlimited for option sellers (either put or call).

In terms of business fundamentals, the company is facing some uncertainties. First, the company has a sizable pending acquisition (of around $20 billion) of Frontier Communications. Frontier is the largest pure-play fiber Internet provider in the United States. I expect the consummation of this acquisition to take time and also likely increase costs in the near term before the synergistic benefits materialize for the long term. Speaking of longer term, I feel quite positive of the prospects here. As an example, the consensus EPS estimates project 4.1% EPS growth rate for the next 5 years as seen. Do not be under-impressed by such a rate. It is already sufficient to make the growth-adjusted valuation very attractive. As a textbook dividend stock, let use the following PEGY ratio that Peter Lynch promoted to illustrate this point.

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

For VZ under current conditions, with a FWD P/E of 9.2x, a growth estimate of 4.1% CAGR, and a dividend yield of 6.3%+, the PEGY ratio is substantially below the ideal 1x threshold.

To close, my thesis in this article is twofold. The use options is part of the investment method used in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) for hedging, income generation, and also leveraging purposes. In the case of VZ under current conditions, we feel the tariff panic has provided an attractive set up for its use given the discrepancy between the extreme IV of its options and its relatively low sensitivity to tariff rate changes. Finally, a word about the rating and also the timeframe this article designates to VZ. First, given that my thesis is based on the discrepancy I see in VZ’s IV and is applicable to both call and put options. As such, the rating I picked for this article is a hold. Second, this thesis is only meant for the near term (say the next ~90 days or so) for the tariff negations to work out.

data.title: Community Financial System: Shares Look Cheap On Margin Expansion Potential
data.description:
As regular readers will know, I've been covering a few smaller, off-the-beaten-path bank stocks recently. These banks typically have a few things in common: they source cheaper-than-average funding; have a history of below-average loan losses; and, perhaps most interesting of all, they have a long track record of delivering consecutive annual dividend hikes, including throughout the last financial crisis.

I am going to round this unofficial series off with a look at Community Financial System (NYSE:CBU). This northeastern bank ticks all of the boxes above, but its stock has struggled quite a bit this year, falling by around 16% on the back of tariff fears.

That move has left Community trading for less than 15x last year's EPS. While that may not look particularly cheap for a regional bank, 2024 wasn't a very good year for Community, and earnings normalization would drive that multiple quite a bit lower. On that basis, I think the shares are decent value right now, and I rate the stock a 'Buy'.

An Overview Of Community Financial System

Community Financial System is the holding company of namesake Community Bank, which operates over 100 branches in the Northeast of the country. The majority of its branches are in upstate New York, though it also has a footprint in Pennsylvania, Vermont and Massachusetts. Community had around $16.5 billion in assets, $13.5 in deposits and $10.5 billion in loans at the end of last year.

Community is a very good bank. It has struggled a bit in recent years, but as the chart below shows, its ROA has typically been higher than the average bank.

There are a few things to like about Community. Firstly, it is pretty good at gathering cheap deposits. Around $3.6 billion of these are in non-interest-bearing accounts, roughly equal to 27% of the overall mix. What's more, it was only paying 1.73% on its interest-bearing deposits last year. As you can see, Community has typically funded its interest-earning assets at a much lower rate than the average bank.

Community also appears to be a fairly conservative lender. Most of its loans are in commercial and residential real estate, weighted slightly toward the latter. Around 17% of the book is in Consumer Indirect lending. These are basically automobile loans originated in dealerships rather than in Community bank branches or online, hence being 'indirect'. While this category produces higher losses, Community typically doesn't lose much on the loans it makes overall. In fact, its annual net charge-off rate has historically been well below average. That includes during the last financial crisis, when its net losses peaked at just 24 basis points.

Potential Upside From Margin Expansion

I said in that last section that Community had struggled a bit in recent years. Last year, for example, it made $3.44 per share in net income, which was only around 7% higher than in 2019. At 1.14%, its annual ROA was the second weakest in the past decade.

One particular issue has been the rapid rise in interest rates, with the average annual Federal funds rate rising by 425bps between 2021 and 2024. On the funding side, Community has actually performed very well. Its cost to fund its interest-earning assets only rose by ~30% of the rise in the Fed funds rate, much lower than the average bank.

The issue has been that its asset yield has also lagged significantly. Whereas peers saw a circa 55% earning-asset pass-through rate between 2021 and 2024, Community's was only just over 30%. That's partly down to its loan book; the bank does more residential real estate lending than peers, and these are long-term, fixed-rate loans. It also reflects its securities portfolio, which was still only yielding around 2% last year. As a result, Community's net interest margin was over 30bps below its ten-year average last year. That may not sound like much, but the bank has over $14 billion in interest-earning assets. A move to its ten-year average NIM would add over 25% to last year's EPS.

The good news is that Community still has plenty of scope for asset repricing, even in a lower interest rate environment, so this issue will resolve itself over time. Meanwhile, funding costs have already topped out now that the Fed funds rate has fallen from its peak.

This dynamic also helps put the valuation into its proper context. Community currently trades for $50.40, putting the prior-year P/E ratio at ~14.7x. That doesn't look cheap for a regional bank, that multiple is based on depressed earnings. It would be much cheaper based on the bank's historical average profitability.

Tariffs do present a bit of a curveball. While 2025 and 2026 consensus EPS points to strong growth, it might be prudent to assume downward revisions. The banking industry faces a lot of uncertainty. Compared to how things looked a few months ago, I now expect lower loan growth and higher credit loss provisioning.

However, there could be quite a lot of upsides here if you can afford a 3-5 year timeframe. As the chart above shows, Community's current P/E is low compared to pre-2023 levels, and like I said, that is based on relatively depressed 2024 earnings. In the meantime, the stock offers an okay dividend. The yield is only 3.65%, but it is covered 1.87x by 2024 EPS. The yield is also comfortably above its ten-year average, another sign that the stock could be undervalued.

If you can look past near-term volatility, these shares could offer a nice combination of margin-driven earnings growth, upward P/E re-rating and dividends.

Conclusion

Community Financial System has had a pretty tough year so far, with tariff fears dragging these shares down alongside most of the market. While it has been under-earning recently, this is a pretty good bank once you get under the hood. For investors able to stomach near-term volatility, these shares could offer a compelling combination of earnings growth, P/E expansion and cash dividends.

data.title: Gold ETF Flows: March 2025
data.description:
March and Q1 in review

Global physically backed gold ETFs1 reported strong inflows in March, totalling US$8.6bn (Table 1).2 This helped drive total Q1 flows of US$21bn (226t) to the second-highest quarterly level in dollar terms, only behind Q2 2020's US$24bn (433t).

North America (61%) and Europe (22%) represented the bulk (83%) of net inflows in Q1. Asia contributed 16% – impressive given that the region’s total assets under management (AUM) only account for 7% of the global total. Additionally, first quarter flows in Europe of US$4.6bn stood out as the strongest quarter since Q1 2020. As a result, and aided by gold’s price increase, AUM reached another all-time-high of US$345bn, representing an increase of 13% in March and 28% through the first quarter.

Additionally, collective holdings rose to 3,445t by the end of March, a 92t addition in the month and 226t higher through Q1, reaching the highest month-end level since May 2023 and 470t shy of the record of 3,915t in October 2020.

Regional overview

North American demand led global flows, adding US$6.5bn and constituting 76% of total flows this month, and US$12.9bn during the quarter. This move higher can be attributed to familiar drivers:

  • the strong price momentum sent gold to above the US$3,000/oz threshold3
  • yields remained rangebound
  • the dollar slipped to levels not seen since last November
  • tariff and war uncertainty provided continued support.

Additionally, equity pullbacks, due to growth concerns and market liquidity worries amid ongoing quantitative tightening, further pushed up investor demand for safe-haven assets.4 Also, increased option activity helped drive US$2.1bn (22 tonnes) inflows at monthly expiry.5

As a result, North American funds posted another strong monthly performance, and the region solidified its significant contribution to global quarterly flows.

Europe saw sizable inflows, drawing US$1bn in March and US$4.6bn during Q1. The rally this month stemmed primarily from the UK, Switzerland and Germany. Although the Bank of England made no changes to its benchmark rate during its March meeting, a cloudy growth outlook, further weighed by US tariff concerns, weak stock market performance and the gold price surge, drove demand higher in the UK. Equally, despite a jump in the 10-year German Bund yield in early March amid Germany’s massive spending plan, investors in Europe continue to add gold ETFs to their portfolios as the ECB’s March cut encouraged further easing expectations6 and US tariff risks loom over the growth outlook.

Inflows were sustained for the fourth consecutive month in Asia, attracting nearly US$1bn in March and US$3.3bn through the first quarter. China and Japan dominated demand in March, both likely driven by rocketing gold price performances, which dwarfed other assets in the month, and roaring global trade policy risks. Additionally, inflationary worries may have helped drive gold ETF inflows in Japan. India saw mild outflows, ending its 11-month inflow streak, as investors may have booked profit. Funds in other regions saw another month of positive demand, albeit only modestly at US$98mn, as Australia and South Africa continue to register gold ETF inflows.

Demand captures changes in global/regional gold holdings; fund flows capture the net amount of money (in USD) that comes in or out of gold ETFs globally/regionally.

Gold trading volumes pullback

Trading activity across global gold markets in March came in at US$266bn/day – broadly in-line with the quarterly average of US$270bn/day. LBMA OTC trading of US$136bn/day, resulted in a quarterly average of US$140bn/day. This marks a notable increase when compared to the 2024 daily average of US$113bn.

Exchange volumes continued to rise in March, with COMEX taking the charge amid the strong gold price performance. Increased option activity supported North American ETF volumes, but global gold ETF activities still fell mildly m/m.

Total net longs of COMEX’s gold futures fell 3% to 804t by the end of March. Net long positions held by money managers remained relatively stable at 599t, down slightly from 605t at the end of February. While money manager net longs declined during the first half of March—likely due to profit-taking—renewed interest driven by US trade policy and geopolitical uncertainties led to increased exposure later in the month. Notably, this rebound followed five consecutive weeks of de-grossing that began in February, bringing net longs just above year-end levels of 764 tonnes.

  • Fund flows represent the amount of money – reported in US dollars – that investors have put into (or retrieved from) a fund during a given period. For more details, see our methodology note.

† 'Global Inflows/positive demand’ refers to the sum of changes of all funds that saw a net increase in holdings over a given period (e.g., month, quarter, etc.). Conversely, ‘global outflows/negative demand’ aggregates changes from funds that saw holdings decline over the same period.
